---
title: "The Apple That Maxed Out"
subtitle: "A cutpoint that works for a whole day's diet quietly breaks on a single food --- and why FRESH keeps two."
author:
  - "Josh Erndt-Marino, PhD"
  - "FRESH's AI assistant"
date: "2026-06-27"
description: |
  On the Healthy Eating Index's fruit component, a raw apple and a slice of two-crust
  apple pie earn the exact same perfect score. For grading a whole day's diet, that is
  the right call. For scoring a single food, it quietly throws away the one number FRESH
  needs most --- and that is why every index in our library carries two cutpoints, not one.
categories: [FRESH, dietary-indices, food-scoring, measurement]
fresh-engines: [Dietary Indices]
topics: [Food Scoring, Measurement]
---

Here is a small thing that turns out not to be small.

The Healthy Eating Index --- the standard scorecard for diet quality in the United States --- scores fruit on a density. Reach at least **0.8 cup-equivalents of fruit per 1,000 calories** and you earn the full five points for the total-fruit component. Less than that, and you slide toward zero. More, and you stay at five.

Run a raw apple through it. In the USDA food databases, a raw apple is about 0.91 cup-equivalents of fruit per 100 grams at 52 calories per 100 grams --- which works out to roughly **17.5 cup-equivalents of fruit per 1,000 calories**.[^numbers] That is about *twenty-two times* the line. It scores a five.

Now run a slice of **two-crust apple pie** through the same component. It comes in around **1.5 cup-equivalents per 1,000 calories** --- still comfortably over 0.8. It also scores a five.

Same number. On the fruit component, the raw apple and the apple pie are **indistinguishable**, even though the apple carries something like twelve times the fruit density. And for the job this index was built to do, that is exactly correct.

## The cap is doing real work

It is tempting to call that a flaw. It isn't --- not at the level the index operates.

The Healthy Eating Index grades a **whole day of eating**. Across a full day your foods average together, and the cap encodes a genuinely sensible idea: *enough is enough.* Past a point, more fruit doesn't make a day meaningfully healthier, and a scoring system that kept rewarding it would let one virtuous component paper over everything else. The cap is there for **balance** --- so no single food group runs away with the score. At the scale of a diet, truncating at 0.8 is not a bug. It is the design working.

This is the part worth sitting with, because it is the trap. The cap is correct. It is correct *for diets.*

(And note what the fruit component never claims: that the apple and the pie are *the same food.* The pie's added sugar, refined crust, and fat all land on other components. The point is narrower and stranger --- on the fruit axis itself, the one place you would expect a raw apple to separate from a pie, the cap makes them equal.)

## Then you ask a single food to wear it

FRESH does not score diets. It scores **foods** --- one apple, one recipe, one swap --- because that is the unit a person actually decides on. "Should I eat this?" is a food-level question, and a dietary index has no food-level answer. It only knows days.

So the move is irresistible: the cutpoint is a density, densities don't care about scale, just point it at a single food's own calories and you have a food-level fruit score. I believed that myself. It is wrong, and the apple is why.

At the diet level, almost nothing sits twenty-two times above the cap, because a day is a mixture. At the **food level**, pure foods routinely blow past it --- a fruit is essentially all fruit. And the moment a food clears the line, the truncation flattens it. The apple's twenty-two-fold surplus and the pie's slim margin both collapse to the same five. The cap, which gently kept balance across a diet, becomes a **ceiling that erases information** on a single food.

That surplus is not a rounding detail. It is the whole game.

## Why the surplus is the whole game

FRESH's scores exist to answer a question the index never asks: *what does this food **do** when it lands in a real person's day?* Drop the raw apple into a fruit-empty diet and it moves the needle hard. Drop in the pie and it nudges --- while dragging sugar and refined grain along with it. The apple's value **is** its surplus density: its capacity to donate fruit to a meal that is short on it.

Truncate at the food level and you throw exactly that away. A food sitting well above the cutpoint loses its ability to *add* in that domain --- the index calls it "maxed out," when in fact it is the most generous donor in the room. You have deleted the one number the simulation runs on.

> A cap that means "enough" for a diet means "you no longer count" for a food. Those are not the same sentence, and a single cutpoint cannot say both.

## So we keep two

This is the design decision underneath FRESH's index library, and it long predates the day I tried to argue the cutpoint was scale-free.

**Every index component carries two cutpoints, by design.** One is the **diet-level** cutpoint --- the index's own, saturating, tuned for balance, the right instrument when you are grading a whole day. The other is the **food-level** cutpoint --- chosen separately, to preserve a single food's additive donate-capacity so the simulation can see how much it actually contributes. Two lines per nutrient. Not redundancy --- two different questions wearing the same name.

It also means real work every time we bring a new component into the library: the diet-level line comes from the published index, but the food-level line has to be *chosen*, trading the index's balance against the engine's need to keep a food's surplus visible. You cannot inherit it for free. The apple and the pie are the proof of why.

## A food is not a diet

The honest version of this is almost embarrassingly simple, which is usually a sign it is load-bearing: **a food is not a diet, and a number built for one will quietly mislead you about the other.**

The dietary indices are not wrong. They are whole-diet instruments doing whole-diet work, and they do it well. The error is in the inheritance --- in assuming a cutpoint that means one thing at the scale of a day means the same thing at the scale of a bite. It doesn't. The truncation that keeps a diet honest makes a food mute.

So we keep two cutpoints, and we say which one we are using and why. That is the small discipline that lets FRESH score the apple for what it really is: not a food that has maxed out, but a food with a great deal left to give.

[^numbers]: Fruit cup-equivalents are from the USDA Food Patterns Equivalents Database (FPED) 2017--2018; energy from the matching FNDDS 2017--2018 food codes --- raw apple (63101000) and two-crust apple pie (53301000) --- computed per 1,000 kcal so the per-100-gram basis cancels. The HEI-2015 total-fruit component awards the maximum 5 points at 0.8 cup-equivalents per 1,000 kcal. A lone food cannot earn a high *overall* HEI --- the apple scores zero on dairy, grains, and protein foods --- so the example is about the fruit component specifically, which is exactly where the cap bites.
